title: GET /health and GET /healthz endpoints
description:
## What
Implement GET /health and GET /healthz (k8s convention alias) endpoints. Each checks DB with `SELECT 1` and checks media dir writable via tempfile. Returns `{"status":"ok","db":"ok","media":"ok"}` with HTTP 200 when all checks pass; returns `{"status":"degraded","db":"error: <sanitized msg>"}` with HTTP 503 when any check fails. All checks must complete within 1 second (use tokio::time::timeout). No authentication required.

## Why
Without a health endpoint, load balancers and container orchestrators cannot determine if the server is ready to accept traffic; degraded responses enable automated failover and alerting.

## Agent team pattern
- Spawn a Research subagent to: review k8s liveness/readiness probe conventions; check what mastodon.social exposes at /health or /healthz; document the JSON shape and HTTP status conventions used by similar Rust/axum services
- Spawn an Implementation subagent to: add /health and /healthz routes to the axum router (no auth middleware); implement db check (SELECT 1 with 1s timeout); implement media dir check (create and delete a tempfile in media dir); build response JSON with sanitized error messages; return 503 on failure
- Spawn a Test subagent to: write tests for (a) healthy server returns 200 with status=ok and both checks ok, (b) invalid DB path (intentionally broken) causes db check to return error and endpoint returns 503, (c) media dir non-writable causes media check to return error and endpoint returns 503

## Test discipline
- Independent oracle: k8s health check convention -- 200 means ready, 503 means not ready; response body JSON is convention, not a standard, but the shape here matches common practice (Prometheus, Grafana, etc.)
- Test approach: inject broken DB connection and assert 503; use test-only config pointing to non-existent media dir and assert 503; assert healthy path returns 200 with all fields present

## Defensive programming checklist
- No `.unwrap()` in handler paths
- Never expose raw OS error messages or file paths in the response body -- sanitize to generic "error: check failed" or similar before including in JSON
- Use tokio::time::timeout for both DB and media checks -- a hung DB must not block the health endpoint forever
- No auth required -- health endpoints must be reachable by load balancers without credentials
- Tempfile cleanup: always delete the tempfile even if write fails (use RAII or explicit cleanup in error path)
